entries.title: *Summa Perfectionis* (pseudo-Geber) — Letterpress Page
entries.creator: Pseudo-Geber (attributed; ed. Nuremberg, Johann Petreius)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1542 Nuremberg (Petreius) edition]
entries.summary:
A letterpress leaf of the Nuremberg *Summa Perfectionis* of pseudo-Geber (Johann Petreius, 1542) — a page of the running Latin treatise (or its index / opening matter), not a figured diagram.

## Iconography
Clean humanist roman type, headed by a running book-title (*Primus*, *Secundus*, *Tertius*, *Quartus*, *Investigationis*, *Mineralia*) and chapter rubrics; some leaves open with a woodcut historiated initial, and the front matter includes a blank/stained flyleaf and a printed index, but these pages carry no apparatus illustration. Recorded honestly as text.

## Significance
The *Summa Perfectionis Magisterii* (c. 1300) is the most important medieval Latin alchemical treatise — long ascribed to the Arab Jabir ibn Hayyan ('Geber') but shown by William R. Newman to be the work of the Franciscan **Paul of Taranto**. Its corpuscular matter-theory and its sober, operational account of sublimation, distillation, calcination and the rest made it foundational. These text pages carry that argument; the handful of apparatus woodcuts are catalogued separately.

## For artists & game designers
A sober sixteenth-century printed page — usable as a manuscript-prop texture or a readable codex leaf for set-dressing.

citations.text: William R. Newman, *The Summa Perfectionis of Pseudo-Geber* (Brill, 1991).

entries.title: Baculus Terreus — The Earthen Stirring-Rod (Apparatus Woodcut)
entries.creator: Pseudo-Geber (attributed; ed. Nuremberg, Johann Petreius)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1542 Nuremberg (Petreius) edition]
entries.summary:
A small technical woodcut from Book II of the Nuremberg *Summa Perfectionis* of pseudo-Geber (Johann Petreius, 1542) figuring the *baculus terreus* — the 'earthen rod' used in sublimation.

## Iconography
Centred on an otherwise text page, a single slender cylindrical rod is shown in profile, captioned *Baculus* at left and *terreus* at right. The surrounding Latin describes how the well-fired earthen rod, pierced with a small hole near its tip, is introduced into the sublimation vessel to test and stir the charge.

## Significance
Tiny as it is, the figure typifies the *Summa*'s hands-on character: the treatise repeatedly pauses to specify a tool or gesture of the bench. Pseudo-Geber's insistence on **experientia** — that the artist must handle, test, and verify — made the work a touchstone of practical [[motif:alchemy|alchemy]] for centuries. William R. Newman's edition attributes the text to Paul of Taranto and stresses exactly this experimental temper.

## For artists & game designers
A humble but authentic laboratory implement — a fired-clay stirring rod with a vent hole. Good reference for a tool icon or an inventory item in a crafting system.

citations.text: William R. Newman, *The Summa Perfectionis of Pseudo-Geber* (Brill, 1991).

entries.title: Four Modes of Distillation — Apparatus Woodcut
entries.creator: Pseudo-Geber (attributed; ed. Nuremberg, Johann Petreius)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1542 Nuremberg (Petreius) edition]
entries.summary:
The principal apparatus woodcut of the Nuremberg *Summa Perfectionis* of pseudo-Geber (Johann Petreius, 1542), illustrating the *primus, secundus, tertius* and *quartus distillationis modus* — four ways of distilling — in Book II.

## Iconography
Four labelled distillation set-ups share the page. Two upper diagrams show a cucurbit fitted with a curved-beak still-head (*alembicus*) discharging into a receiving flask (*ampulla recipiens*); a lower-left diagram sets a vessel over a fire of flames (*ignis*, *receptaculum*); the lower-right shows a flat alembic-and-receiver. Marginal Latin captions name the parts — alembic, lapides (the luted joints), concha, vas recipiens.

## Significance
The *Summa Perfectionis*, the most influential Latin alchemical treatise of the later Middle Ages (c. 1300, long ascribed to the Arab Jabir / 'Geber' but argued by William R. Newman to be the work of the Franciscan **Paul of Taranto**), is famous for its precise, operational descriptions of laboratory practice. This woodcut renders that precision visible: it is among the earliest printed diagrams of [[motif:distillation|distillation]] apparatus, a direct ancestor of the furnace-and-alembic plates of the later alchemical tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A genuine period schematic of stills and a flaming furnace — ready reference for an alchemist's-bench crafting UI or for modelling believable distillation props.

citations.text: William R. Newman, *The Summa Perfectionis of Pseudo-Geber* (Brill, 1991).

Clean humanist roman type, headed by a running book-title (*Primus*, *Secundus*, *Tertius*, *Quartus*, *Investigationis*, *Mineralia*) and chapter rubrics; some leaves open with a woodcut historiated initial, and the front matter includes a blank/stained flyleaf and a printed index, but these pages carry no apparatus illustration. Recorded honestly as text.

## Significance
The *Summa Perfectionis Magisterii* (c. 1300) is the most important medieval Latin alchemical treatise — long ascribed to the Arab Jabir ibn Hayyan ('Geber') but shown by William R. Newman to be the work of the Franciscan **Paul of Taranto**. Its corpuscular matter-theory and its sober, operational account of sublimation, distillation, calcination and the rest made it foundational. These text pages carry that argument; the handful of apparatus woodcuts are catalogued separately.

## For artists & game designers
A sober sixteenth-century printed page — usable as a manuscript-prop texture or a readable codex leaf for set-dressing.
